source.title: Structural Damage - Crane Operator Rope Access Incident at Stampede TLP

context.region: Gulf of Mexico, Green Canyon Block 468

event.top_event: Uncontrolled descent of rope access technician
actions_taken: Injured person evacuated by helicopter to onshore hospital
Safety standdown of all rope access work called
Rescue performed by alternate rope access team
First aid treatment provided by onboard medic
event.summary: On May 31, 2023, at 09:00 hours, a Rope Access Technician (RAT #2) employed by Southey Contracting experienced an uncontrolled descent of approximately 90 feet while inspecting the Seawater Reject Caisson (SWRC) on the Stampede tension leg platform. The technician's working rope slipped through an improperly threaded I'D descender device at the anchor point. During the fall, the technician's left leg struck a SWRC support clamp, causing lacerations and ligament damage. The fall was arrested approximately 8 feet above the waterline when the anti-error catch safety feature of the I'D engaged near the stopper knot. The injured person was rescued by an alternate rope access team and evacuated via helicopter for medical treatment.
recommendations: Discontinue use of Shunt devices as personal backup devices and replace with alternative backup devices
Implement refresher training for all RATs on safety and supervisory procedures
Implement Advanced Rescue Training for supervisory personnel
Review and enhance Stop Work Authority procedures
Mandate risk assessment and equipment requirement evaluation for all work scope changes
Provide dedicated and suitable rescue kits with third set of ropes for emergency rescue
Require supervisors to maintain direct line of sight with RATs during rope positioning
Amend rope access procedures to include suitable knots (e.g., alpine butterfly) attached to structure to prevent slippage of descending/retrieval devices
Develop and implement robust rope access permitting checklist
Ensure buddy checks and function testing are completed by different team members
Implement equipment tracking and identification system for all rope access gear
Provide materials and procedures to mitigate wet work conditions from SWRC discharge leak
Install directional carabiners per manufacturer recommendations for all I'D descender installations
Secure/tie-off all anchor point devices with knots to prevent accidental lowering
Ensure stopper knots are installed directly behind anchor point I'D devices
key_phrases: mechanical bypass of Shunt backup device

hazards.name: Work at height - rope access operations
Corroded seawater reject caisson with structural integrity concerns
Wet work environment from SWRC discharge leak

threats.name: Improper installation of I'D descender device at anchor point
Absence of directional carabiner to protect anti-error catch
Failure to secure/tie-off anchor point devices
Inadequate supervision of rope access technicians
Failure to complete function testing and buddy checks
Improper installation and securing of rope protector
Use of Shunt backup device without proper understanding of failure modes
Inadequate training on equipment operation and manufacturer recommendations
Work scope change without risk assessment and equipment evaluation
Wet work conditions affecting technician performance and equipment function

consequences.name: Uncontrolled descent of rope access technician
Impact with SWRC support clamp causing leg laceration and ligament damage
Potential fatality from fall from height
Injury to rope access technician requiring evacuation and hospitalization

controls.name: I'D descender device with anti-error catch safety feature
controls.lod_basis: Mechanical safety device designed to arrest falls

supporting_text: the safety catch within the I'D was bypassed when the excess rope was laid to the front of the device as opposed to below or behind it
the anti-error catch safety feature was illustrated when excess rope was run through a directional carabiner

the working rope whipped over a cable tray underneath the anchor point and shifted the angle of the rope feed direction through the I'D giving the anti-error catch safety feature an opportunity to function

controls.name: Shunt backup device (personal rope grab)
controls.lod_basis: Mechanical backup device designed to engage during falls

supporting_text: RAT #2 maintained grip on the unsecured rope pro above his Shunt which mechanically bypassed and defeated the Shunt continually as it slid down his safety rope
the IP overrode his Shunt by pulling his unsecured rope pro down over it
by maintaining his grip on the rope pro, the IP changed the angle of the Shunt's rope grab mechanism and prevented the Shunt from performing its designed function

the natural human reflex is to increase the grip on the cord and therefore reduce the likelihood of the cord to be pulled from the hand, additionally this natural reflex may override any conscious action to open the hand and release the cord

controls.name: Directional carabiner installation at anchor point
controls.barrier_role: Ensures proper rope feed direction through I'D
controls.lod_basis: Manufacturer requirement to enable anti-error catch function

supporting_text: he did not pass the excess working rope from either of the anchor ID's through directional carabiners to ensure function of the anti-error catch
WARNING: the anti-error catch will not work unless the rope passes through a directional carabiner on the anchor
Pictures and video provided by Southey exhibit that directional carabiners were not installed at either Stampede or during Drop Testing

controls.name: Anchor point device tie-off/securing with knot
controls.lod_basis: Manufacturer recommendation for releasable anchor configuration

supporting_text: he did not tie-off all of the anchor devices to prevent accidental lowering of RAT's
One simply puts the handle in the 'work positioning' position and completes the setup by tying-off the device
For the specific use of making a releasable anchor with the I'D, it is imperative that the device is also tied off to prevent accidental lowering of the on-rope worker

controls.name: Stopper knot at end of working rope
controls.barrier_role: Prevents worker from descending off end of rope
controls.lod_basis: Standard practice for work at height operations

supporting_text: the stopper knot at the very end of the IP's working rope above him approached the anchor point I'D
the teeth in the anti-error catch bit down within approximately one meter of the stopper knot at the top end of the working rope
A closely located a stopper knot is a best practice that would have limited the fall distance to under 1-meter

controls.name: Rope protector (rope pro) installation and securing
controls.barrier_role: Protects rope and prevents mechanical bypass of backup device
controls.lod_basis: Equipment design to prevent rope damage and device bypass

supporting_text: RAT #2 did not fully tighten the rope pro and did not remove the slack from his ropes
the rope pro was found near the water line at the end of the IP's safety rope
by maintaining his grip on the rope pro, the IP changed the angle of the Shunt's rope grab mechanism and prevented the Shunt from performing its designed function
Had the rope pro been installed properly, the IP would not have been able to continually mechanically bypass his Shunt

controls.name: Function testing and buddy checks of rope access equipment
controls.barrier_role: Verification of equipment integrity and proper installation
controls.lod_basis: IRATA training requirement and industry best practice

supporting_text: RAT's #1 and #2 did not conduct independent assessments of the anchor point installations prepared by their Southey Supervisor
Neither function testing nor buddy checks were completed on each other's personal RA kits
Despite Southey's policy and IRATA training requiring function testing and 'buddy checks' of the RA equipment prior to beginning work, Southey did not comply
the IP's deviation from standard practice when failing to complete function testing of his equipment prior to the job

controls.name: Supervisor direct line of sight and oversight of rope access technicians
controls.lod_basis: IRATA training requirement and supervisory responsibility

supporting_text: the Southey Supervisor then walked away from the work site and left three Level 1 RAT's unsupervised
a procedural deviation occurred when double-checks and functional test were not completed by different members of the work group due to inadequate supervision
Require supervisors to maintain direct line of sight with RATs during rope positioning

controls.name: Adequate fall rescue plan and dedicated rescue equipment
controls.barrier_role: Rapid rescue capability in emergency situations
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response requirement for work at height

supporting_text: Southey only had two sets of ropes and a third was not available for emergency rescue
Southey's fall rescue plan did not include installation of a third set of ropes to be available in the event of an emergency
the rescue plan depicted that in an emergency situation, the Southey Supervisor would be the only person available to haul up an IP back onboard
An adequate fall rescue plan was not in place prior to beginning work on May 31, 2023
Had the other RA Team not have been present, prompt rescue would not have taken place

controls.name: Training on equipment operation and manufacturer recommendations
controls.barrier_role: Knowledge and competency in safe equipment use
controls.lod_basis: Competency requirement for rope access work

supporting_text: Southey's training on both the I'D and Shunt were proven to be inadequate
Southey did not comply with the manufacturers' recommendations on the proper way to feed rope through the I'D or installation of a directional carabiner

the Southey Supervisor exhibited a knowledge-based error as he thought the ID was error-proof, but the anti-error device only works if the slack end of the rope is run in the opposite direction as the load side

controls.name: Work scope change risk assessment and equipment evaluation
controls.barrier_role: Identification of hazards and control requirements for changed work

supporting_text: on the morning of May 31, 2023, the MSIP Lead called Stampede to speak with the Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) RAT Supervisor and informed the RAT Supervisor the job scope changed from inspection of fixed lifting equipment to additional inspection of the SWRC

on the morning of May 31, 2023, despite the Southey Supervisor expressing frustration with the challenges associated with SWRC inspection, the MSIP Lead made the decision to shift the work scope back to further inspection of the SWRC
a risk assessment following changes to the work scope would have resulted in the requirement for a dedicated and suitable rescue kit

controls.name: Mitigation of wet work conditions from SWRC discharge leak
controls.barrier_role: Reduction of environmental hazards affecting technician performance

supporting_text: Pictures and video taken during CVI captured images of the wet work conditions created by the SWRC discharge leak continuously cascading water 90' below to the waterline
The Southey NDT RA crew made an effort to stay dry by donning waterproof, hooded coveralls and attempting to seal them off by taping their pant legs around their leather boots
The Southey RAT's reported they experienced burning sensations in their eyes, nose, and mouth and suffered discomfort

Although Hess provided material to wrap the leaking SWRC perforations during SWRC inspections on May 3 and May 11, a lack of continuity of communication between the TIP and MSIP Inspection Teams led to Southey unnecessarily facing repeating work in wet conditions during the succeeding inspection
On May 31, 2023, the date of the uncontrolled descent, no attempt was made to wrap the SWRC and mitigate the wet work conditions for Southey

controls.name: Visual observation and communication of incident by witness
controls.barrier_role: Early detection and notification of emergency situation

supporting_text: RAT #3 witnessed the IP's uncontrolled descent and called the Southey Supervisor over the radio to inform him of the incident
Another RA team working at Stampede, witnessed the incident from their work site near RAT #3 at the NW Hull ToC

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
